transcript:
Ben Zhou: Awesome. Huge place.

Lily Liu: It's a stadium, it's not just a stage.

Ben Zhou: Awesome. Yeah.

Lily Liu: So actually we did a fireside chat last year in Singapore as well, so happy to reprise that. So Ben, what's new in the Bybit world?

Ben Zhou: Well, a lot of things. So, I think this year for me is really focused on compliance and getting license. I think the exchange space, since we launched in 2018, I think the first three years, we were, you know, like we discussed in the backstage, we were focused on building product, doing innovation, it's really good fun time. And I think for the last two years, it was really about licensing because the whole space... we're the first one that got the UAE SCA license, so we're also here in Abu Dhabi. And then we have the MiCA license from Europe. We overall, Southeast Asia, Thailand, Vietnam, Indonesia, everywhere is getting ready for onboarding onto crypto. So I think the role of the exchange now is really how do we connect to the local banks and become the place for people, the massive people that haven't been onboarded into crypto, so when the... to own their first, let's say, hundred dollars into crypto. So that's kind of our job and our focus now.

Lily Liu: Got it. And then product-wise, it's much more than an exchange now. I know the Bybit card has been a huge success for you guys. And so product-wise, how are you expanding?

Ben Zhou: Oh yeah. So a lot more focus on payment, especially in emerging markets. Our biggest growth this year is in Latin America, CIS region, as well as Africa. So we have license in Brazil, Argentina, Turkey, South Africa. So for these places, the... you see the adoption rate, especially the unbanked population, they will treat crypto and the crypto exchange like a bank. And basically we give them crypto card, they can use it, and then at the same time they have Earn program, they can even buy structured product, and things like that. So, yeah, yeah.

Lily Liu: Got it. And...

Ben Zhou: What is your focus been? Has it been kind of shifting, you know, compared to the first part, a few years, and this, you know?

Lily Liu: Yeah, I think that we're fundamentally builders of infrastructure, first and foremost. We joke that... well, Solana really is just a GitHub repo, and we joke that we as Foundation are a human interface for a GitHub repo. And so we're not building companies, we don't operate companies, we advocate for a GitHub repo, which is kind of like weird because there isn't like really another precedent for that. But then I think that over time, the different... there's kind of like this progress to how you build an ecosystem. There's an order of operations. Of course, if the network doesn't exist, there's nothing for anyone else to do. And then the most important thing after you have a functioning network is to attract developers. No developers, no apps, nothing for users to do. So we think about it as you build the network, attract developers, that essentially has been like the core thing, network and app developers for basically the last four or five years. And then what happened this year is there's this huge broadening into institutional investor audience, into institutions issuing assets, and so this whole stablecoin, RWA type of thing. So I feel like we added so many different audiences this year, and it's way beyond just developers, as startups and developers as it has been for the last few years.

Ben Zhou: And you know how my world is really... I spend half of my time meeting different regulators. I was just in Indonesia meeting JK, talking about licensing. Does this regulation framework and environment change affect you at all? And how is the Solana Foundation interact with regulators? Yeah.

Lily Liu: Yeah. We certainly interact with regulators a little bit different from how you guys operate, or as operating businesses interact with regulators. We were the first blockchain to set up a policy institute, the Solana Policy Institute. We've got a full team, about six people in DC, where focused on US regulation given that opened up and went from a fairly hostile place to a fairly collaborative environment. But we care about different issues than you might, because we're not seeking a license. We're not an operating business. But we do care about things like, you know, are developers going to be prosecuted for writing code? That's something we're going to care disproportionately a little bit more about than an exchange might. And I think that when we go talk to, let's call it, countries... we talk to countries about less about any individual piece of regulation, but it's more, you know, as a blockchain, we support countries with town formation, capital formation, and this whole theme of internet capital markets. So we certainly go talk to countries around the world, but it's less about regulation, it's a little bit more policy and also sort of how you grow the economy together.

Ben Zhou: Yeah, kind of ecosystem focus.

Lily Liu: Yeah, ecosystem. Yeah. Countries are ecosystems too.

Ben Zhou: Yeah. Yeah. Okay. So, you know, Bybit, we launched a DEX in Solana called By-Real, you know. And the By-Real team is here today. They gave us this little thing. By-Real team. So, you know, I also talk to my team, it's kind of a... a good thing and also a curse when a centralized exchange, especially a big one, launches a project. I think the team wants it to be independent and people see By-Real as a real DEX rather than a sub-thing out of the centralized exchange. And obviously, I think the team is trying quite hard to attract projects in a completely decentralized way. Basically, they don't view Bybit resources as part of the requirement. What is your view on that? And what's your advice to give if it's a project incubated by the centralized exchange?

Lily Liu: Yeah. I think that especially in the earlier days, which is right now, people are going to see it as having been incubated by Bybit. And that is part of the appeal. That is part of the startup part of it. But ultimately, like any product, I feel like it's got to win on being a competent, good to very good product itself first. So over time, like the balance of those things has to shift. A product where it's like, if people ever only see By-Real as Bybit's Solana DEX project, then it hasn't really done its job, right? And it hasn't earned its way out of that. Because that's kind of the starting point, but then that's also there's like a half-life to that characterization. So that's a challenge for the By-Real team, I suppose. I think they're doing quite well right now, but you got to like out-earn that over time, right? And grow at a pace which is faster, hopefully multiples faster, than the half-life decay of kind of being this incubated project.

Ben Zhou: Yeah. Yeah.

Lily Liu: How do you see that?

Ben Zhou: Um, yeah, I think... just what the team... we're discussing what is the key strengths of By-Real. Number one is probably the liquidity. We want to make sure that DEX has really good liquidity, and we already start to see a lot of the order even being routed to By-Real because the liquidity and the spread is quite good. But again, like you said, innovation is a key. And that's not only for DEX, for centralized exchange. Bybit is the second in the world, and I keep telling the team we cannot beat the number one by copying Binance, you know. We need to start to think what we could do on our spot market, on our Earn program, that really solves client's need rather than simply try to price war and these type of things. It never wins. So I think for our next focus on the centralized side is probably RWAs. We focus quite a bit on tokenized stock. And this I think cross to By-Real as well. By-Real also wants to focus quite a bit on RWAs. We think it's the future of the industry. Yeah, yeah.

Lily Liu: And so how do you see the Bybit Alpha, By-Real, and then Bybit, or more broadly, the centralized and decentralized space converging, complementing one another? You know, obviously the fact that you guys did incubate By-Real means that you see this as being positive sum game. I'm curious how you think about that reinforcing one another.

Ben Zhou: Yeah. So wallet... it's... so we actually started the wallet project for the first two years, and then for me, I just see huge investment, but I don't see stickiness. From exchange side, we see a lot of good wallet out there, and we feel like, to me, it's extremely hard to compete on a wallet front because people don't just choose a wallet, they choose what the wallet can do. Whether it's the airdrop it offers, sure project, or they want to get onto Solana to do certain things. So, for example, if you want to trade on Hyperliquid, if Hyperliquid only supports one wallet, most people would just use that one wallet. I don't see a competitive stickiness on wallet, and this is why we kind of stopped developing the wallet project. But DEX is different, I think. DEX is real, it's liquidity, people always need a DEX, it's where trading happens. And the relationship between the CEX to the DEX, I think is not really competition. It's part of the journey as being an infrastructure. We see ourselves as an infrastructure. We get so many licenses so we can connect to the local banks, like here in UAE, maybe the First Abu Dhabi Bank, so that clients can deposit AED into Bybit to start to explore into crypto. But the moment they explore and they become more confident, they will need a wallet maybe to explore into DEXes and even in Solana different projects, so as they become more confidence. So we see it's really a maturing phase. And we still host majority of the crypto curious people who just get into the industry, and then we ship them off maybe to you guys after they become more native, you know. Yeah, yeah.

Lily Liu: Got it. So you see DEXes as even being like a pro product?

Ben Zhou: I do see that. Yeah, I mean, we interact with a lot of clients all over the world. A DEX to most people... we focus on so much on user experience. On a CEX exchange, every single button is designed by a purpose. Why this button is here, why is this round? It's because clients would by default click it easier, right? But the moment they go to, I think Solana Web3, it's a very open world. They need to have basic knowledge of how to explore, how to navigate, understand what's going on, and even using a self-custody wallet, right? So we at least still play that part of curating that, make them more confident, I think. So that's our job, you know. Yeah, yeah.

Lily Liu: Got it. Got it. And how do you see the... do you see chains as being kind of like decentralized liquidity that is going to be more significant over time than a centralized exchange?

Ben Zhou: Oh yeah. We've seen significant shift of liquidities moving from centralized exchange to DEX over the last two years, right? Not only spot, but also derivatives. We see more liquidity happening in Hyperliquid and these type of derivative DEXs. So I think people are definitely more confident in exploring DEXes. Like I said, you know, once they have been trained, they will be shipped off into that area because I think it offers more freedom, more privacy obviously, and definitely more choices, right? So I think the trend is going to continue to happen. But again, our job is still the un-crypto people, which is even bigger. I think the world is still... I think the next question for you probably is what is the biggest challenge? I still think it's probably the throughput issues. If the real... we have a lot more users onboarding into Solana, can we really handle them in the billions, you know, in the tens of billions? And that's kind of our job to bring these people in, you know, and you might face the same problem, you know.

Lily Liu: Uh, I think that by nature of being decentralized, the ecosystems built on top of open infrastructure, they are inherently going to be... because it's open, there's going to be a lot of stuff, and there's a little bit of discovery and fragmentation of experience. And what you see with... I think one place where you see this is a tradeoff between when you have permissionless asset issuance, you have five different forms of BTC. And so you fragment liquidity. And so what do you choose? Do you choose the permissionless nature of asset issuance and say, well, you know, fully asset neutral, so okay, well, you know, that's life, you've got ten different forms of BTC. And then, you know, some people see that as a arbitrage opportunity for solvers, for developers to build a solver network around that, and intents... okay, so some people think, well, that's a great opportunity to solve that through technology and it's arbitrage, right? So you just keep things maximally permissionless. And then there's another view of the world which is, well, there's liquidity is a public good for the whole ecosystem. So if you unify that liquidity through some coordination mechanism, then that's actually better. And I think that in the chain world, you'll see quite a variety of different perspectives on this. And so when it comes to something like Bitcoin, I think we probably have, you know, at least five or six different wrappers of Bitcoin through different technologies. So then the question is, okay, well if you really want to scale for a billion users, is it really reasonable to expect those people are going to, you know, DYOR on, you know, the technical intricacies of this wrapper versus that? And so I think this is a bit of a UX decision to be made, and this is like, it's a sliding scale of judgment.

Ben Zhou: Yeah. Yeah. No, I agree. So on our side, we are trying to also focus quite a bit on throughput, TPS, and make sure we can expand. I think the October 11th massive drop, that, you know, whole... it really was a test of centralized infrastructure. And even with that, we see the downtime of a single cloud can crash a lot of the centralized exchange. So that's our focus is to have multi-disaster recovery strategies so that the availability of the exchange stays. Yeah.

Lily Liu: Yeah. I mean, attending that certainly motivated us as well. I think, you know, decentralized infrastructure shifted I think people's tradeoff perspective. That, well, if you have decentralized infrastructure, that can actually perform, and you know, there was one that performed rather well that day... Solana did very well that day... and you have more transparency throughout the stack, transparency on principally where the oracles... how oracle data is fed. So, you know, we'll see. I think that you have these defining events that will change the way people perceive tradeoffs. Maybe there will be something that shifts it in the other direction. So, anyways. So, yeah. Well, thanks for being a great partner.

Ben Zhou: Thank you. Thank you. Thanks. Thanks. We'll continue to support. Thank you.

# Solflare unveils self-custody crypto debit card
Today, Solflare, the gateway to the next era of finance, has partnered with Mastercard, to launch the Solflare Card, the first true self-custody debit card on Solana.
[]()019 November 2025[Be the first to comment](#comments)
#### External
This content is provided by an external author without editing by Finextra. It expresses the views and opinions of the author.
Seamlessly linking to users' Solflare Wallets, the new digital card allows users to make instant debit card payments with USDC while maintaining full control of their assets, without preloading or prior conversion. The Solflare card will initially be available to those in the UK and European Economic Area (EEA), before being rolled out in additional regions.
Featuring built-in biometric verification, fraud detection mechanism, real-time notifications and 3DS, the Solflare Card is designed to enable users to spend crypto as fiat, instantly, securely, and globally. By partnering with Mastercard, the Solflare Card will enable seamless stablecoin and crypto transactions at more than 150 million Mastercard-accepting merchants worldwide.
“Crypto is no longer simply a speculative asset class, and we want our 4 million+ strong community to use crypto in whatever way suits their daily needs”, said Filip Dragoslavic, Co-Founder and Co-CEO of Solflare. “With the Solflare Card, we are making it faster, safer, and easier to participate in the crypto economy. Crypto spending shouldn’t feel risky or overly complicated. The Solflare Card brings control back to users, with security and simplicity built-in.”
Christian Rau, senior vice president of Global Partnerships, Digital Assets, Blockchain and Fintech Enablement at Mastercard, said: "Mastercard is committed to integrating digital currencies into the broader payments ecosystem. By working with partners like Solflare, we further that mission and empower users through safe, simple and instant access to their crypto assets – truly bridging the gap between digital asset ecosystem and everyday payments.”
The Solflare Card is linked to users’ non-custodial Solflare wallet, meaning users maintain control of their private keys while Solflare manages the rails. This keeps users’ private keys secure, never exposed or transferred, while each transaction is signed directly (with the user’s approval) on the card, supported by three-factor authentication, including biometric verification, a PIN, and the physical card itself. Upon launch, Solflare cards are compatible with Google Pay, with Apple Pay coming soon, for fast, highly secure, contactless payments.
Vidor Gencel, Co-Founder and Co-CEO of Solflare, added, ““With over 115K of our loyal community members signed up for the

sources.title: Bitlayer Monthly Report — October 2025
sources.snippet:
Bitlayer Monthly Report —October 2025 | Bitlayer BlogBitlayer Monthly Report —October 2025 | Bitlayer Blog
![Bitlayer Monthly Report —October 2025](data:image/png;base64,iVBORw0KGgoAAAANSUhEUgAAAAMAAAACCAQAAAA3fa6RAAAADklEQVR42mNkAANGCAUAACMAA2w/AMgAAAAASUVORK5CYII=)# Bitlayer Monthly Report —October 2025
[![Bitlayer](https://blog.bitlayer.org/assets/images/authors/bitlayer.png)](https://x.com/BitlayerLabs)[Bitlayer](https://x.com/BitlayerLabs)04 Nov 2025
![share](https://blog.bitlayer.org/assets/images/icon/common/icon-share.svg)
* * Announcement
* Monthly Report
Welcome to Bitlayer!
October marked a period of accelerated growth for Bitlayer, highlighted by significant ecosystem expansion and institutional adoption.
A key development was the entry of YBTC family assets into the**Solana ecosystem**, coupled with the launch of innovative YBTC.B Vault products like the YBTC.B-wBTC pool. These offerings have successfully attracted users across multiple chains including**Avalanche and Sui**, signaling growing cross-chain demand. This momentum clearly indicates the continued evolution of a robust Bitcoin capital market built around YBTC.
![](https://blog.bitlayer.org/_bl_news_events/images/20251104/1.png)
On the institutional front, BTR’s integration with**Coinbase Prime**represents a major milestone in Bitlayer’s path toward institutional adoption, opening doors for future participation from traditional financial entities.
Our global presence was further strengthened through extensive engagement at Korea Blockchain Week, where both co-founders delivered insightful presentations on critical industry topics including:
* *The future of Bitcoin with OP\_CAT*
* *Scaling DeFi through innovation*
* *Bitcoin as the ultimate asset and Solana as infrastructure*
These developments collectively demonstrate Bitlayer’s growing influence in shaping the future of Bitcoin-native finance. Let’s dive into this month’s developments!
## 📊Network Metrics Snapshot
* **Bitlayer YBTC Family Data**: $214.78M (Source:[DeFiLlama - Anchor BTC Protocol](https://defillama.com/protocols/anchor-btc))
* **Transaction Volume**: 89.47M (+7.95% MoM)
* **Cumulative Addresses**: 773,552## 🤝Chains Integration
Bitlayer is now[bringing](https://x.com/BitlayerLabs/status/1981741242154430900)Bitcoin liquidity to the Solana ecosystem through the fully verified Bitlayer–Solana bridge, unlocking new yield and DeFi opportunities across chains.
📝Official YBTC.B Contract on Solana: 3VcKofugG1SPJmjuiEZCJL5mk1JkyqGZ19ByeMWXVWfK
![](https://blog.bitlayer.org/_bl_news_events/images/20251104/2.png)
## 🏎YBTC.B Expansion
YBTC.B serves as a universal cross-chain liquidity standard for Bitcoin, bridging EVM and non-EVM ecosystems and enabling seamless deployment of native BTC across multiple DeFi networks.
Recent Integrations:
* Now[live](https://x.com/BitlayerLabs/status/1978753595534459222)on Folks Finance with a new YBTC.B vault offering rewards up to 6.7% APY.
![](https://blog.bitlayer.org/_bl_news_events/images/20251104/3.png)
* YBTC.B - wBTC pool[live](https://x.com/BitlayerLabs/status/1983510872610816258)on Turbos Finance, empowering BTC-backed DeFi on Sui.
![](https://blog.bitlayer.org/_bl_news_events/images/20251104/4.png)
* YBTC.B–sat.YBTC.B pool is now[live](https://x.com/BitlayerLabs/status/1983739046565441571)on Momentum, empowering smarter Bitcoin yields.
![](https://blog.bitlayer.org/_bl_news_events/images/20251104/5.png)
To date, YBTC.B is now actively supported across Sui, Solana, Ethereum, BSC, Avalanche, Plume Network and Starknet. Now you can explore YBTC.B products on leading protocols including:[Folks Finance](https://x.com/BitlayerLabs/status/1958207858031694135),[Momentum](https://x.com/BitlayerLabs/status/1957465911834935448),[Cetus Protocol](https://x.com/CetusProtocol/status/1960620728958771430),[Turbos Finance](https://x.com/BitlayerLabs/status/1983510872610816258),[LFJ](https://x.com/BitlayerLabs/status/1942450205342703940),[SatLayer](https://x.com/satlayer/st

## **Solflare Partners With Mastercard to Launch Solana First Self-Custody Debit Card**
* **Payments
* **20.11.2025**08:55 am
![Solflare Partners With Mastercard to Launch Solana First Self-Custody Debit Card](https://financialit.net/sites/default/files/financial_it_-_news_template_updated_2_312.png "Solflare Partners With Mastercard to Launch Solana First Self-Custody Debit Card")
Today,[Solflare](https://www.solflare.com/crypto-card/), the gateway to the next era of finance, has partnered with[Mastercard](https://www.mastercard.ie/en-ie.html), to launch the Solflare Card, the first true self-custody debit card onSolana. Seamlessly linking to users' Solflare Wallets, the new digital card allows users to make instant debit card payments with USDC while maintaining full control of their assets, without preloading or prior conversion. The Solflare card will initially be available to those in the UK and European Economic Area (EEA), before being rolled out in additional regions.
Featuring built-in biometric verification,frauddetection mechanism, real-time notifications and 3DS, the Solflare Card is designed to enable users to spendcryptoas fiat, instantly, securely, and globally. By partnering with Mastercard, the Solflare Card will enable seamlessstablecoinandcryptotransactions at more than 150 million Mastercard-accepting merchants worldwide.

tags.impact_reason: Bybit focusing on compliance and payment solutions with crypto cards in emerging markets could increase access to Solana.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a hybrid bank vault as the Semantic Anchor because the story is about Bybit, a centralized exchange (CEX), bridging the gap between traditional/centralized finance and the on-chain, decentralized world. The vault symbolizes the security and institutional nature of a bank or CEX, while its dual-material construction—half traditional steel, half futuristic crystalline circuitry—perfectly represents the 'Hybrid Finance' thesis. I included the diegetic text 'HYFI' laser-etched onto the vault's locking mechanism. This acts like a model name for this new financial gateway, directly embedding the core concept into the object itself.
visual_idea.description: A massive, circular bank vault door stands slightly ajar in a dark, minimalist space. The door is conceptually split in two: one half is thick, brushed stainless steel, representing the traditional centralized exchange. The other half is a semi-translucent crystalline structure with pulsating purple and green circuits visible inside, symbolizing the Solana-native DEX. Where a manufacturer's name would be on the central locking wheel, the word 'HYFI' is cleanly laser-etched into the steel. Through the opening, instead of gold bars, a vast, glowing network of interconnected blockchain data streams recedes into an infinite digital background. The lighting is high-contrast, with a cool, ethereal glow spilling out from the vault's interior.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A monumental, massive, circular bank vault door stands slightly ajar, towering within a dark, minimalist, expansive space. The composition emphasizes verticality (3:4 aspect ratio), with ample negative space above, making the vault feel epic in scale. The vault door is conceptually divided: the left half is crafted from thick, brushed stainless steel, symbolizing a traditional centralized exchange, showcasing realistic material detail. The right half is a captivating, semi-translucent crystalline structure, glowing with an internal pulsating network of purple and green circuits, embodying a Solana-native DEX. On the central locking wheel, the word 'HYFI' is cleanly laser-etched directly into the brushed steel, subtly catching the light. Through the narrow opening of the vault, an immense, glowing network of interconnected blockchain data streams extends infinitely into a deep digital background, acting as a gateway to an unseen dimension. The lighting is high-contrast and dramatic, with a cool, ethereal glow intensely spilling outwards from the vault's interior, sharply illuminating the edges of the brushed steel and crystalline lattice, while the surrounding minimalist space recedes into deep, mysterious shadows, creating a cinematic atmosphere. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, ultra-photorealistic, cinematic, high dynamic range, photoreal detail.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a hybrid bank vault as the Semantic Anchor because the story is about Bybit, a centralized exchange (CEX), bridging the gap between traditional/centralized finance and the on-chain, decentralized world. The vault symbolizes the security and institutional nature of a bank or CEX, while its dual-material construction—half traditional steel, half futuristic crystalline circuitry—perfectly represents the 'Hybrid Finance' thesis. I included the diegetic text 'HYFI' laser-etched onto the vault's locking mechanism. This acts like a model name for this new financial gateway, directly embedding the core concept into the object itself.
visual_idea.description: A macro shot focusing on the intricate seam where the two halves of the vault door meet. The frame captures the stark textural contrast between the cold, machined grain of the brushed steel and the complex, self-illuminated crystalline lattice of the other half. The focus is sharp on the point of fusion, where microscopic digital energy arcs between the two disparate materials, symbolizing the connection point between centralized and decentralized systems. No text is visible in this version.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A hyper-detailed macro shot, 1:1 square aspect ratio, focusing intensely on the intricate, perfectly symmetrical seam where the two halves of a futuristic vault door meet. The composition features a tight, centrally focused macro frame, entirely filled with the stark textural contrast. One half displays the cold, machined grain of brushed steel, exhibiting a matte, industrial sheen with subtle metallic reflections. The opposing half reveals a complex, self-illuminated crystalline lattice, pulsating with an ethereal internal glow and razor-sharp intricate geometry. The focus is critically sharp on the precise point of fusion, where microscopic digital energy arcs, crackling and shimmering with vibrant intensity, bridging the two disparate materials. This visual connection powerfully symbolizes the interface between centralized and decentralized systems. No text is visible. The scene is lit with dramatic, high-contrast cinematic lighting, casting deep, defined shadows and creating luminous highlights that accentuate the unique textures and the radiant glow of the crystalline lattice against the brushed steel. 8k, V6 realism, ultra-photorealistic, photoreal detail, realistic materials, natural color grading, high dynamic range, octane render, raytracing, cinematic contrast, shallow depth of field with a precise focus on the seam, hyper-detailed.

editorial.headline: Bybit Pivots to 'Hybrid Finance' with Solana-Native DEX Byreal
editorial.standfirst: CEO Ben Zhou admits the 'pro' trade is moving on-chain, positioning his centralized exchange as a fiat gateway while incubating a new decentralized liquidity layer. The move signals a formal acceptance of the 'Hybrid Finance' thesis by one of the world's largest exchanges.
key_takeaways: Bybit incubates **Byreal**, a Solana DEX utilizing RFQ and CLMM to bridge CEX liquidity with DeFi transparency.
Zhou redefines the CEX role as a "fiat gateway" for the unbanked, ceding advanced trading to on-chain protocols.
The "October 11" cloud outage is cited as the tipping point proving decentralized infrastructure's superior reliability.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
In a candid fireside chat at Solana Breakpoint 2025, Bybit CEO **Ben Zhou** delivered a stark admission: the era of the centralized exchange (CEX) as the ultimate destination for crypto liquidity is ending. Zhou outlined a strategic pivot where Bybit serves primarily as a compliance and fiat onboarding layer—the "first $100" for users in emerging markets like Brazil and Turkey—while sophisticated trading volume migrates to decentralized infrastructure.

This thesis underpins the launch of **Byreal**, a Bybit-incubated DEX built on Solana. Unlike generic AMM forks, Byreal integrates **RFQ (Request for Quote)** and **CLMM (Concentrated Liquidity)** mechanisms designed to bridge CEX-level depth with on-chain transparency. Zhou emphasized that while the project leverages Bybit’s resources, it is designed to "out-earn" its incubation status, operating as an independent protocol to capture the flow of institutional capital moving on-chain via products like **xStocks** (tokenized equities).

The strategic shift comes in the wake of the "October 11 massive drop," an event Zhou cited as a stress test that exposed the fragility of centralized cloud infrastructure. While single-point failures took down major CEXs, Solana’s decentralized network remained operational. Solana Foundation President **Lily Liu** seized on this, noting that decentralized infrastructure has crossed the threshold from theoretical resilience to superior performance, validating the network's role for high-throughput applications.

For institutional investors, the signal is clear: the "Mullet" thesis—fintech in the front, DeFi in the back—is becoming formalized. Bybit’s aggressive push into local licensing and card programs in the Global South is the customer acquisition funnel; **Byreal** and the Solana ecosystem are the settlement layer where the real value will increasingly reside.
